author: @TheRestOfTheWeek
text:
Friction, inertia, ferromagnetic properties rod, and energy state.
The steel rod does not move because the system is already in its lowest possible energy state, the rod is longer than the coil. If you put the rod half way in to the coil you might have a better chance the rod will move. The rod becomes a magnet in itself when a current flows through the coil, the rod will settle in the centre of the magnetic field of the coil depending on the strength of the field. It also depends on the amount of iron(ferro) in the steel rod, and if friction and inertia are overcome. 

The iron/steel construction gives a more concentrated magnetic field, there is no amplification of the total magnetic field strength or energy for that matter. The only reason why the coil moves(friction and inertia are negligible) is because of the permanent magnetic field that attracts or repels the electromagnet depending on the direction of the current, no free energy here!

author: @eblocha
text: I'm not getting how this is free energy. When you run the current from the battery through the solenoid, it creates a magnetic field in the solenoid. One side of the field will be +, the other -. The - side attracts toward the + side of the magnet in the apparatus, pushing the solenoid to that side. When you flip the battery, it flips the magnetic field, pushing the solenoid to the other side. It didn't move outside the apparatus because there was no magnet to be attracted to.

author: @maxigakuo
text: First is to thank you for sharing this. Don't give up sharing. On ideas you have. One the first experiment with coil, battery and a core. You were creating a magnet. You should actually expect it to move anywhere. 30volts won't. Second experiment. Well all know about magnet. N and s attract s and s repels. So when you apply even a quarter of a volt you give the coil identity. South or north and thats what makes the movement. Not amplification of anything.

text: I know there is a low power consumption 555 timer. They are ''Cmos 555'' but i am not sure once again if it is possible to power it from a joule thief. I think 320 miliamps is a big amount of energy for the output flyback of a joule thief. Still, i would be curious about that...
